responseSet.title: Strephosymbolia Reconsidered—A Re-Study of a Specific Inhibition of the Use of Visual and Auditory Verbal Symbols
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Strephosymbolia Reconsidered—A Re-Study of a Specific Inhibition of the Use of Visual and Auditory Verbal Symbols

Maurice Walsh

Maurice Walsh

Maurice N. Walsh
436 North Roxbury Building
Beverly Hills, Calif.

Maurice Walsh  

SUMMARY

The analysis of a case of severe strephosymbolia is described. Rosen's (1955) finding of a lack of synthesis between the ordinal (phonetic) aspects of words and the visual aspects and the origin of this lack in primal scene fantasies and the pattern of object relationships is confirmed as well as his finding that precocity of the "reading readiness stage" of ego apparatus development is important etiologically. The importance of pregenital factors in the formation of early language, and the decisive role of pregenital conflicts in the predisposition to the operation of oedipal factors are described.

The opinion is expressed that regression rather than an initial failure to synthesize the ordinal (phonetic) and the visual aspects of words is

594

responsible for much of the symptomatology, this being contrary to the opinion expressed by Rosen (1955). While milder strephosymbolic syndromes may more or less clear during adolescence, the opinion is expressed that some inhibition in reading and spelling persists, at least in many if not most cases.

Optimism is not warranted in the treatment of such severe cases as that of the patient described in this communication, without psycho-analytic therapy, five years of intensive analysis being required for the resolution of his strephosymbolic syndrome. The opinion is expressed that a considerable, but unknown, number of school drop-outs are due to the discouraging effects of severe strephosymbolia. Education of teachers and speech therapists in the recognition of the strephosymbolic syndrome is recommended. Further research into the therapy of the syndrome is urgently needed.

responseSet.title: Crying at Weddings (And) "When I Grow Up"
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Crying at Weddings (And) "When I Grow Up"

Robert Dorn

Robert Dorn

Robert M. Dorn
450 North Bedford Drive
Beverly Hills, California U.S.A.

Robert Dorn  

A very charming and happy five-year-old girl cried while attending her first wedding, but was unable to say why. She made only one comment at the time: "Weddings are so sad." Her father discussed this with me, and was told to listen carefully for further comments that might clarify her sadness. Shortly thereafter she went to her second wedding, and afterwards carried on an animated conversation with her father. He made careful notes which I discuss further on. One sentence from this conversation was as follows: "When I grow up, I'll let my little girl be anything she wants to be."

We are used to grown-ups being emotionally "touched" by the "beauty of the wedding and the bride". Women are particularly prone to show their tearful feelings at this time. However, men also will admit similar emotions, although usually only with reticence.

Margaret Mead (1949) refers to crying at wedding ceremonies. She feels it is a universal phenomenon which strikes at the very core of life, and that our marriage ranks along with our birth and our death, as a time when barriers are broken down. For a moment, we see ourselves and others like us without the walls, clothes, and other impediments to clear-sighted evaluation of ourselves and other human beings. She feels that a better knowledge of our inner selves would make our "self" picture less fantastic and more real. What is this inner self Mead refers to? What is this "core" of life all about?

In this paper I will use clinical material, a play, Our Town by Thornton Wilder (1938), and psycho-analytic knowledge to describe this central theme, common to all human beings.

Contrary to Feldman's statements (1955), these truths are painful to the child as well as the usually "sensible" and "mature" adult. Both adult and child attempt to prevent the impingement of unpleasant realities. The early ego uses primitive forms of defence against it (e.g. invulnerability, omnipotence, and denial, with tenacious clinging to the pleasure principle). As we grow older the more typical defences of the maturing ego appear, but the primitive defences do not disappear entirely. One childhood representation of this complex defence is the phrase: "When I grow up". Play (both solitary and group types) incorporates many features of this phrase. Society aids the individual in his defensive struggle by supplying rituals and institutions of a group participatory type that are outwardly festive and foster identifications with the "lucky" persons.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Hypothesis of a Deglutitive (Prenatal) Stage in Libidinal Development

Ivan Milakovic

Ivan Milakovic

Ivan Milakovic
Lenjinova 9
Sarajevo
Yugoslavia.

Ivan Milakovic  

SUMMARY

According to proposed hypothesis, we are in a position to delimit one phase of libidinal development which precedes and is a preliminary to orality. Its elements are manifest in later developmental stages and may emerge in regressive states. These elements have a share in the genesis of certain symptoms, such as stammering, affective tics of swallowing, anorexia, depression,

81

etc, and they reveal the lack of satisfaction of tendencies towards resignation, to the realization of homeostatic quiescence, and the abandonment of the extraverted attitude. These tendencies may be transformed into various depressive, masochistic, conversion, and other phenomena, culminating in suicidal fantasies. The sudden eruption of deglutitive phase tendencies in their original form occurs in states of collapse or shock.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalysis of Dreams

By Ángel Garma. (Chicago: Quadrangle, 1966; London: Pall Mall, 1967. Pp. 334. 30s.)

Jaime Tomás

Jaime Tomás

Jaime Tomás  

This book is a translation into English of the fourth edition of a book on dreams, written in Spanish, which has been greatly valued in Spain and the Latin American countries. One of its characteristics is that it is illustrated with drawings of dreams executed by the dreamer. It also contains other kinds of drawings such as reproductions of plastic art works which permit the study of the symbolic processes which intervene in the genesis of dreams.

After giving a general view of unconscious mental functioning, the book describes 164 different dreams together with the dreamer's associations and the respective latent meanings. The dreams quoted in the first part are those of a patient, from the beginning of his treatment to its conclusion. These allow the reader clearly to follow the latent situations of conflict causing this patient's neurotic sysmptoms to appear disguised through the day residues in the transference relation to the analyst, and pave the way for the therapeutic process. The drawings of the dreams referred to, spontaneously undertaken by the dreamer during his treatment, clearly show his associations with the manifest contents and underline how they led to the discovery of unconscious contents. This keeps the reader's attention alert when otherwise it would be apt to stray somewhat inasfar as dream material is concerned, due to the fact that he identifies himself with the dreamer in his state of sleep.

Most of the remaining dreams are given according to the order of their latent contents. Thus, among the dreams with genital contents, are in the first place described those referring to the male and female organs, and thereafter those provoked by the different aspects of genital activity. The explicative index of the symbols referring to each dream, found at the end of the book, is a valuable contribution. It is followed by a second index of the symbolized contents, with mention of each of the symbols used.

The psycho-analytic explanations given for each dream depart from the patient's associations but, in order to make them more comprehensible, also lean on another type of material such as literary fragments where the same elaborative processes occur. Psycho-analytic literature is also given in resumé in the book, although not in as much detail as in the third Spanish edition, three times as voluminous as the English version.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Jung Really said

By E.A. Bennet. (London: Macdonald, 1966. Pp. 185. 15s.)

James Hood

James Hood

James Hood  

The publication of Jung's collected works in twelve commodious volumes throws doubt on the claim which is implied in the title of this short book. Or is the rest all padding? The scrupulous reader will still want to judge for himself, and as he sets out to explore the dark realms of Jung's thought he must often wish—at least I did—that there were a clear sighted guide to lead him on his way. Having read the opening chapters of Memories, Dreams, Reflections —and surely there could be no better introduction to Jung's life's work than the intimate recollections of the man himself—it is difficult to avoid the metaphor and the thought that Jung was not so much a product of his time as of his place of birth. In spite of his enormous erudition—and for Freudians especially his original studies of religious, and particularly Eastern thought, are recommended reading—Jung probably remains for most analysts a curiously and conservatively rooting animal, who does not travel well from his native Switzerland. The present volume does not provide a guide and, while respecting its author's conscientiousness and devotion, I did not find his attitudes helpful; indeed, together with a pedestrian style, they make for a distinctly dull little book. Its importance lies in its reminding us that we cannot afford to neglect Jung if we wish to analyse patients for whom religion still matters.

472

[[RunningHead]]

Jahrbuch Der Psychoanalyse

Beiträge zur Theorie und Praxis. Vol. 4. Edited by Käte Dräger, Alexander Mitscherlich, Horst-Eberhard Richter, Gerhart Scheunert and Edeltrud Seeger-Meistermann. (Bern: Hans Huber, 1967. Pp. 267. Fr./DM. 38.)

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner  

Together with Communications of the German Psycho-Analytical Society (DPV), the list of its members and their scientific publications (from 1963 to 1967), reports of its training institutes and conferences, the fourth volume of this year-book contains further theoretical and clinical contributions of interest. Apart from (translated) essays which have already appeared elsewhere (W. Muensterberger on the "Creative Process and its relation to the Loss of Object and Fetishism", J. O. Wisdom on the "Psycho-Analytical Theories of Melancholia", and J. Klauber on "Three Typical Stages of Transference in the Analysis of Neurotic Depression"), the theoretical part of this annual contains Peter Fürstenau's essay on sublimation, the continuation from the Vol. 3 of Klaus de Hoppe's essay on transitional objects, and W. Loch's on the "Possibility of a Shortened Psycho-Analytical Therapy". Fürstenau emphasizes both the differences between reaction-formations and sublimation and the importance of the role of sublimation as a critical function. He is in opposition to the views (of Kubie, Greenacre and Kris) which do not attribute therapeutic value to sublimation, and those who (like Glover) regard sublimation as an outcome of successful phobia. With Fenichel, the author connects sublimation with identification and wishes to distinguish "non-repressive" sublimation, i.e. a non-violent levelling out between the aims of the drive and the ego, and those reactive, repressive structures which shelter under the cloak of apparent normality but which in fact are, under the aegis of successful drive-control, nothing but inconspicuous neurotic manifestations. There is a useful review of psycho-analytic definitions of sublimation in this paper. In Hoppe's view many patients experience the analyst less as a transference and more as a transitional object, which, if the phenomenon is temporary, proves to be useful, whereas the permanent re-cathexis of a transitional object (now in the form of the analyst) would increase the patient's transference resistance. These patients distinguish an "official" and a "private" world, the latter being full of transitional objects and phenomena, which may become the matrices of illusion, in which case fetish-formation could occur. The self-image of the borderline patient is transitional in nature. His object-relations are transitional (Modell); the analyst's fees become transitional objects for him (Gedo); the fixed ideas of the obsessional are internalized transitional objects (Solomon); transitional objects into which the child projects his own self can become "secondary transitional objects" (Stevenson); and, finally, it is noted that there will be a split relationship of the child to the mother if she presents him with a "fetish": the relationship to the real and that to the "fetish"-mother, which latter is then neither a transitional object nor a valued toy (Sperling); hence, the fetish has, dynamically, been condensed from the repeated preoedipal object choice and from a transitional phenomenon (Parkin). The author, listing all these views, agrees finally with Gillespie: that this process can happen only in the genital stage of the boy. Loch discusses Balint's "focal therapy" with a view on the possible shortening of treatment in some analytical cases. The focal conflict on which the analyst can base his interpretations emerges already in the first interview between the interactions of therapist and patient. In order to bring about successful interpretations in focal therapy, transference and countertransference levels have to converge both in respect of the (fictitious) normal and the infantile-narcissistic ego and the neurotically cathected object-relations.

[[RunningHead]]

Culture Against Man

By Jules Henry. (New York: Random House, 1963; London: Tavistock, 1966. Pp. xiv + 495. $7.95; 45s.)

J. Padel

J. Padel

J. Padel  

Dr Henry is an anthropologist experienced both in fieldwork and in teaching; and Culture against Man is based on observations made by himself and by workers trained by him, of American society. "Pathways to Madness", Chapter 9, is for psycho-analysts the most interesting chapter of the book. It is a record of a week which Henry himself spent in each of two families (the "Portmans" and the "Rosses") at a time when a psychotic child of

609

the family was away in hospital. Henry notes the impact of each parent on the other children and also of the father-mother relationship. This is unique material, on which we can apply and retest our theories about the genesis of personality disorder and of madness. The sort of questions implicit in the chapter are: What is the impact on a 15-month child of being regarded by the mother as a faecal monster; or on a four-year-old of being felt to need minimal food? It seems to me that the mother in the Ross family unconsciously felt her child to be still within her and that her husband shared this fantasy. This is a conceptualization which the author himself does not make, and it leads to a further question: How do we conceptualize such impacts? It is clear from Henry's study that there must be big components in the child's self image that are taken over unconsciously from the parents' unconscious fantasies of him. The sort of work that the chapter embodies will surely play a major part in the development of object-relations theory and in future psychopathography.

The book is, however, much more than Chapter 9. It is "a major argument … that culture is a unified whole, even unto psychosis and death"; and although it is American culture with which Henry is concerned, there is much in the book which is valid for all modern societies. Henry shows how explicit American cultural rules have become. In Part I he quotes these rules from contemporary documents, such as "Create more Desire!" (from an advertisement in the New York Times), and points to the state of "technological drivenness" to which this sort of rule has led, to "dynamic obsolescence, the drive to make what is useful today unacceptable tomorrow", to the reliance of the economy on fear (of failure, of humiliation, of becoming obsolete) and to a craving for fun to offset the loss of identity and of self-respect. Advertising is promoted to the status of a "Philosophical System", certainly of an ethical system, advertising men being self-styled a group of "dedicated men and women". In Part II, Henry shows the effects of these forces upon the different group making up the society—families, schools, teenagers, and so on. He gives material from his observations to show the effects of the system on the parents who cannot create the family in which their children can find what they need and their use of the system to provide substitutes. He shows too how children in school are conditioned to pseudo-discipline and against creativity. Part III is concerned with institutions for old people and provides evidence of inhumanity in even the best of them.

Dressur Oder Erziehung, Schlagerituale Und Ihre Gesellschaftliche Funktion. (Breaking-In or Education, Beating Rituals and their Social Function.)

By Klaus Horn. (Frankfurt, Suhrkamp, Schriften zur Psychologie und Soziologie, 1967. Pp. 127.)

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner  

This booklet, which otherwise is not more than a sharp critical review of some Neo-Nazi ethno-sociological essays (mainly by one W. Hävernick), could have had a greater scientific appeal if instead of just censoring the practice of corporal punishment in education (which it does alongside Freudian tracks, praising Aichhorn's pioneer work in Vienna in the twenties) it had given the interpretation of beating as a ritual and had also centred on the schizophrenic origin of authoritarian educational theory instead of only mentioning it.

The book's most valuable chapter is about the "murderer behind the desk", who is able to hide his cruelty behind anonymity, an attitude which the author connects also with obsessive thinking: taboos, rituals, isolation, and aggression. The more complex the regulations in society, the more paralysing they become and the less relief they can provide. Narcissism, inner emptiness, and lack of pity characterize persons who are obsessed with it and whom, with Adorno, the author calls the "manipulative type". They are pseudo-rational, can deal with things only under a technological or an organizational aspect. These people are infantile even if otherwise they may be highly specialized experts.

610

responseSet.title: Homosexuality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Homosexuality

L. Hornstra

L. Hornstra

L. Hornstra
Slingerweg 9
Wassenaar
The Netherlands.

L. Hornstra  

Introduction

A psychological phenomenon is at the same time a social phenomenon because man, the bearer of the psychological phenomenon, is a social being. Whether a psychological phenomenon is discerned as a social phenomenon depends upon various factors: the number of individuals that show the phenomenon, the way in which they show it, and the amount of fear it arouses in those who observe it.

In considering homosexuality we are concerned with the actions and reactions of homosexuals, with the reactions of society, and with the scientific investigation of the psychological phenomenon. In the future it will be found necessary to consider these three subjects together, because dealing with them separately may lead to mistaken conclusions.

Most of the actions of homosexuals appear to be nothing more than attempts at justification, and the manner in which these attempts are made is of great importance to the comprehensive investigation. In all probability, the homosexual is badly in need of the feeling that he is put at a disadvantage, and the abolition of all discrimination (assuming that to be possible) would be anything but welcome to him.

The reactions of society are also extremely important. In recent years and in some western countries, especially The Netherlands, attitudes have undergone great changes. The antipathy to and defence against homosexuality are ancient; they are directly related to the total past and present attitude towards sexuality. It is indeed a question whether it is homosexuality that is so strongly rejected. And no investigation has been made of the reasons for the present increase in tolerance and interest. That homosexuality itself is an important social phenomenon is evident from its increased frequency; other disturbances such as exhibitionism, voyeurism, necrophilia, coprophilia, etc. do not seem to show any similar increase.

Space does not permit consideration here of all three areas of the problem (the actions of homosexuals, the reactions of society, and the scientific investigation of homosexuality). I prefer, at present, to restrict myself to the last; I shall therefore discuss the schematic defensive structure of some forms of homosexuality, taking as my basis both analytical and non-analytical material.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Voraussetzungen, Mechanismen Und Grenzen Des Psychoanalytischen Prozesses

(Hypotheses, Mechanisms and Limitations of the Psycho-Analytical Process). By Wolfgang Loch. (Bern: Hans Huber, 1965. Pp. 78. Fr./DM.14.50).

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner  

The author, who lists 196 works in his bibliography (no index attached), painstakingly

122

confronts Freudian views and their exegeses according to his own system of presentation. This constitutes both the virtue and the shortcoming of his project. He has a gift of compiling, and also producing, good formulations. Clear insights are not lacking. Besides highlighting the problems of resistance, transference and counter-transference, he makes a point of tracing structural modifications as well. However, he makes a ponderous task of encompassing everything important in his treatise; it is not by chance that he is most impressed by Freud's having "taken the patient seriously". He takes his own need towards perfectionism too seriously. He discusses the problem whether there is a hierarchy of defences, or whether they should be divided into successful versus unsuccessful, repressed versus not repressed, "lower" versus "higher" ones, and whether they should be regarded as stages in ego development. He discusses the nature of counter-cathexes and how far they limit therapeutic work. He quotes the six methods of "taming the instincts" by Nietzsche which he regards as a separate category of resistance, and he does not forget to mention the "normal defences": "acceptance, reflection, judgment and appropriate action". He lists the "semantic" and "propositional" aspects of interpretation, and draws attention to the connexion between interpretation and the formation of the ego. Again, he distinguishes between "anaclitic-diatrophic" and "neurotic" transferences, the "therapeutic" transference and the transference of the ego ideal or the superego. He sees three limits to analytical interpretation: (i) limits of individual creativity, (ii) physiological-biological limits of primary repression and (iii) an "iatrogenic" limit. The conditions of communication (pre-conditions of interpretation) are: (i) "Mit-Sein" (togetherness, empathy), (ii) the dialogue, and (iii) the "dialectic presence" of the non-gratifying analyst. We could continue this abstract ad libitum.

This book, although it also represents some recommendable virtues, suffers from most of the shortcomings of German scholarly works. It struggles with heavy terminology—owing to a predilection for the obscurities of Heideggerian language, e.g., he speaks of a "Seinkönnen des Seienden" ("ability to exist of the one with the status of being in existence") where we would simply say: "Wesen und Identität" (essence and identity). True, the author's thinking, on the whole, is psycho-analytical, but it seems quite unnecessary to drag in Jaspers, Phenomenology, Husserl, Existentialism, Hermeneutics and Wittgenstein. One is not quite sure whether it is a compliment to the psycho-analytical authors who are also quoted and referred to, to be mentioned in comparison and parallels with representatives of other disciplines, though most eminent, or whether it is an embarrassing and often incongruous juxtaposition. It certainly is confusing for the reader.

[[RunningHead]]

Psyche.

Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse und ihre Anwendungen. Vol. 20, No. 1. Ed. by W. Hochheimer, A. Mitscherlich et al. (Stuttgart, E. Klett, 1966. Pp. 80. DM. 4.80.)

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner  

The Psyche, hitherto known as the German periodical for "Psychological and Medical Anthropology" (Zeitschrift für psychologische und medizinische Menschenkunde), as of its twentieth year will change its sub-title and, with it, its character to a monthly periodical for "Psycho-analysis and Applied Psycho-analysis". This new venture should be warmly welcomed by all friends of psycho-analytic thinking, as the first attempt to fill the gap and continue in the tradition laid down by Freud in the "Internationale Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse" which met its tragic end twenty-five years ago.

The Editorial of the first number introduces the historical reasons for the necessity of these alterations. Psyche started in 1947 in a period of disorientation caused by the artificially enforced isolation from the West of more than a decade of German thinking, among other fields as well as in that of psycho-analytical research. Only slowly has the focus of interest returned from hesitant eclectic researches in depth psychology to a more decisive, more commanding reorientation towards psycho-analysis proper. This interest has not been marshalled; it grew in a natural, one could say "organic" way, similar to the helio- or hydrotropism of plants.

This new periodical is the outcome of true development. German psychology has found its way back to the nourishing and rich supplies provided by the international confluence of the twentieth century's intellectual sources. The name of the journal's editor-in-chief, A. Mitscherlich, is a guarantee in itself for the value of the periodical.

Fraternal psycho-analytical associations were ready to join forces and help this new reorientation.

123

responseSet.title: The Concept of Identity: By David J. de Levita.  (Paris:  Mouton.  1965.  Pp. 209.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Identity

By David J. de Levita. (Paris: Mouton. 1965. Pp. 209.)

Peter Lomas

Peter Lomas

Peter Lomas  

There are several words which may be used to describe that which we regard as intrinsic to us: being, self, person, individual, identity, and so on. Which word we select depends perhaps on arbitrary choice. In the field of psycho-analysis it has happened that this entity has been given its most adequate expression by Erikson and that he has selected the term Identity with which to label it. The author of The Concept of Identity is openly and profoundly influenced by Erikson's work.

De Levita traces the use of the term in philosophy from Plato onwards, regarding William James as the originator of the modern concept, and follows this with a most lucid and illuminating account and critique of the work of Erikson and others within the psycho-analytic movement who have focussed attention on identity.

In the latter part of the book the author develops a concept of identity based on role, defining it as the "cluster of roles one is enacting." He believes that

the assignment of identity means a particular kind of reduction in a relationship. In a way, the people who want to identify me treat me as if I were a thing with properties (by which it is identifiable) rather than as a living object. They reify me.

Identity is "the sum of the reifications". One has no identity on an uninhabited island. Individuality, in contrast with identity, is "the way in which I fulfill my roles, and … the unique combination of roles which I call mine."

De Levita advances his idea with great clarity and, in so doing, is very informative on the part which role-relationships play in personal development, but, in my view, he has not done justice to that part of life—which might best be called spontaneous—in which one's role is unimportant. Surely it is primarily during times when role is forgotten, when people in a relationship are unselfconscious, that belief in one's uniqueness grows? Moreover such experiences do not carry the disadvantage of interchangeability (as the author himself notes) inalienable from fulfilling a role. What De Levita has formulated would perhaps better be called "role-identity". We have still to find our way to a concept which would include uniqueness and mutuality, spontaneity, and unpredictability.

The Schlegel-Tieck Prize

James Strachey has been awarded the 1966 Schlegel-Tieck Prize for his translation of the works of Sigmund Freud. The award has been made for Volume 1 of The Standard Edition of The Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud which (with the exception of the Index volume) completes the set of 24 volumes, the first of which appeared in 1953.

The prize of £300, plus a week's visit to the Federal Republic of Germany at the expense of the Germany Embassy, is awarded annually for the best translation of works published in the German Federal Republic during the twentieth century.

A panel of three judges decides the award, this year composed of Eric Mosbacher, Christoper Holme, and Norman Denny. The presentation is made later in the year by the German ambassador at the German Embassy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Brief Separations

by C. M. Heinicke and I. Westheimer (New York: Int. Univ. Press; London: Longman, 1965. Pp. 355. $ 8.50. 63s.)

Frederick Stone

Frederick Stone

Frederick Stone  

This book contains an account of a research project undertaken by the Child Development Research Unit at the Tavistock Institute of Human Relations, London. The aim was to study the effects on young children of short periods of separation from their parents. For this purpose they selected ten children in their second and third years admitted temporarily to residential nurseries, and as a contrast group chose ten children matched for age and sex, who remained in their own homes with their parents during comparable periods of time. The behaviour of each child was observed before and during the separation and for at least twenty weeks after reunion, using a coding system of recording the child's behaviour especially in interaction with other people, as well as objects. Standardized doll-play sessions were also included at set times during the separation. These more objective data were supplemented by the nurses' accounts as well as the observers' own clinical descriptions. Identical methods of observation and recording were used for the contrast group. Care was taken to test observer reliability.

The stimulus of this project is the earlier work of Anna Freud and Dorothy Burlingham, James Robertson, and of course John Bowlby himself who is Director of the Child Development Unit and whose help in the planning and execution of the project is acknowledged by the authors. "While all research must be inspired and guided by theory", states Bowlby in the foreword, "it is desirable, in the exposition of data, that theory be reduced to a minimum. In this the authors of the present study have been meticulous". This is so, and very refreshing was the resulting mode of presentation for this reviewer. The procedures are clearly described, the findings stated with necessary reservations, the literature reviewed fully in a rather original fashion by introducing the relevant sections under appropriate parts of the whole text, and listed in a concluding bibliography.

This is a serious piece of work involving a great deal of disciplined labour in a field where so much is still impressionistic or biassed by theoretical orientation. The problems of research design are great, however, and it is questionable whether in this project they have been satisfactorily solved. The authors themselves recognize frankly some of the limitations of their design to some extent imposed on them by practical considerations. For example, the children besides being separated are also in new surroundings; and the matched group, experiencing neither of these factors, is an imperfect control. A satisfactory control group should theoretically be identical with the sample with the exception of one variable. Moreover it could be argued that the fact that at a time of crisis (admission to hospital of the mother) the family's method of coping which involved the toddler's being sent away from home to a nursery, may well signify something about this family. In seven out of ten separated cases the mother was about to give birth, so that the question arises whether the pre-separation behaviour was related to this fact, and the post-separation behaviour to the presence of a new baby in the house. With some ingenuity the authors dent with these criticisms, but doubt lingers. Again, although the control children are matched for age no mention is made of developmental status, and this could clearly be of relevance with regard to speech sphincter control, and other areas.

[[RunningHead]]

Sinn Und Gehalt Der Sexuellen Perversionen. (Meaning and Essence of the Sexual Perversions)

By Medard Boss. (Bern, Hans Huber, 1966. Pp. 142. Fr./DM.14.80.)

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner  

This is the third, corrected and enlarged, edition of an "existentialist" monograph, heavy with Heideggerian ideology and terminology, based however on the teachings of Binswanger who, once having been a follower of Freud, has retained many of his psycho-analytical concepts and ideas. The author chooses, however, to accept Dalbiez's dispraise of Freud. Freud is quite frequently mentioned, though mainly in order to refute his theories, even if, strangely enough, some lip-service is paid to his merits. In contradistinction to the approach of Freud, who assumed a polymorphous perverse stage to exist in the child's early development, the author believes only in the "pervertibility" of the child and tries the opposite way of understanding the pathological in perversions, by comparing them to the normal heterosexual experience of adult love, illustrating his tenets with eight case histories including one case of fetishism, one of coprophilia, one of scopophilia, one of sadomasochism, three of homosexuality (two of them females) and one of kleptomania. The selection is not explained. The author blames the "out-of-tune" relationship of the pervert with the world as the main cause of his sexual aberration. In his view, it is with his aggression, his impulse to split and his need for distortion that the pervert tries to ensure a "loving togetherness" in the world which is everybody's and so also his, primal aim towards the love-object. It is also his, and not only the normal individual's, main objective to "lovingly taking part" in the existence of the partner. To give a few samples of the author's theories: sadism aims at the "breaking through" of a desire toward a "loving togetherness"; in kleptomania the same is achieved through the close contact and immediacy between the patient and the material object (which the author regards as a sexual perversion, a form of fetishism, if there are no obsessive actings-out). The fetishist and the coprophile are able to overcome the barrier of moral, fear, disgust and shame, but only through certain "notches" of the partner's corporality; the barriers have to be "exploded" in kleptomania, sadism and scopophilia, etc. Suicide is also a violent and radical "breaking through" of the same order, etc., etc.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Development of the Mind

By Jeanne Lampl-de Groot. (New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1965; London: Hogarth and Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 1966. Pp. 373. 45s.)

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

This collection of papers leaves one in no doubt as to the massive contribution which Jeanne Lampl-de Groot has made to psycho-analysis. The papers which are most original, such as those on "The Evolution of the Oedipus Complex in Women" (1927) or on "Masochism and Narcissism" (1937) at the same time display a high capacity for integration of knowledge—of theoretical exactitude with clinical insight, of preoedipal development with the crystallization of pathology in the phallic phase, of psycho-analysis with other branches of psychology or with biology. The contributions, theoretical and clinical alike, are marked by a striving for systematic clarity. Apart from an interval for the war years, Lampl-de Groot's productivity has been steady for more than thirty years.

Though most of the papers concern some aspect of the development of the mind the title of the book does not quite succeed in suggesting the contents. It is a collection of papers for the most part concerned with aspects of the development of the mind but not a systematic exposition. To some extent, as the publishers say, it is a guide to the development of psycho-analysis. The pre-war contributions largely concern the problems of feminine development and of masochism. Lampl-de Groot shows great sensibility for the small child's awareness of its smallness, and it was her major contribution to enlarge the understanding of the girl's castration complex, previously based on anatomical differences, in terms of a convincing psychology of disappointment based also on the inevitable failure of her active object-seeking relationship with the mother. Moreover Lampl-de Groot never forgets that penis envy is not the exclusive prerogative of the female.

Equally impressive is her partial elucidation of the problem of masochism in terms of a defence against narcissistic trauma ("Masochism and Narcissism", 1937). The basis of this view may be illustrated by the girl's preference for the fantasy that her mother removed her penis because it saves her from the painful realization of "deficiency" from the beginning. This theme is further elaborated, to show how masochistic degradation aids the preservation of a secret omnipotence in "Superego, Ego Ideal and Masochistic Fantasies" (1963). In the development of this theme Lampl-de Groot can be seen elaborating Freud's description of the woman's penis-envy as "bedrock".

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Healing the Sick Mind

By Harry Guntrip. (London: Allen & Unwin, 1964. Pp. 223. 13s. 6d.)

Kevin Leddy

Kevin Leddy

Kevin Leddy  

This book is a sequel to Dr Guntrip's You and Your Nerves, which was the expanded version of a series of broadcast talks published in 1957. The author states explicitly that he has designed this book "for those among the reading public who have become aware of and concerned about the grave problem of mental health". Guntrip offers us a scholarly, philosophical and humane examination of the problem considered in its broadest possible context.

His thesis is that fear and not guilt is at the core of psychological distress and psychiatric illnesses. This fear is not so much a fear of strong impulses as of our infantile weakness, littleness and helplessness in the face of an environment which either failed to give the facilitating support we needed as infants or else was positively threatening. For Guntrip the importance of the mother-child relationship lies not so much in the satisfaction of the baby's instinctual needs as in its role of laying a firm foundation for an adequate ego-growth and stable feelings of self identity. Guntrip is insistant that the root cause of nervous illness is to be found in the deep-seated fears and feelings of weakness derived from infancy. He abscribes a crucial role to fear of weakness in the development of the schizoid character. His description of the schizoid personality and its predicaments is both sensitive and insightful. He has a deep sympathetic understanding of these people and their problems and it is just this that he advocates to all psychotherapists.

In the latter part of the book Guntrip makes a strong and convincing case for the wider use of psychotherapy in the treatment of mental illness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Die Krankheitslehre Der Psychoanalyse (Psycho-Analytic Psychopathology)

Edited by Wolfgang Loch et al. (Stuttgart: S. Hirzel, 1967. Pp. 290. DM.26.)

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner

L. Veszy-Wagner  

It was two years ago that Loch published his work on the "Hypotheses, Mechanisms and Limitations of the Psycho-Analytical Process". This new work not only avoids all the pitfalls of the previous one but presents us with a concise and comprehensive textbook, keeping abreast with the newer results of the relevant literature. Although on a more modest basis than Fenichel (presenting only about a third of the quantity in Fenichel's 1945 English edition of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Neurosis) it successfully undertakes the same tremendous task as Fenichel's first German edition has done, that is: to mould what constitutes the corpus of Freud's teaching into a well-balanced, systematized form, without violating the original, chronological, almost organic alterations which it had to undergo in its gradual development during Freud's long and ever active life. At the same time it gives account also of those positive additions with which Freud's followers have enriched his original teachings. It could be regarded as a moot point whether the inclusion of some of the views of the "neo-Freudian" school was justified as well. However, in view of the commendable impartiality and the unbiased critical spirit of this compendium, this should perhaps be answered in the affirmative. Fenichel's was the great accomplishment; all those who had emulated him since, the pressures of theoretical advancements making this necessary, had only to enlarge and bring new points of view into focus, re-arranging rather than altering or correcting the magnum opus. Loch achieves a similar task with tact, taste, and a good sense of proportion. The advantage of Loch's work lies therefore, not only in its contemporaneous publication, but also in its having achieved the goal: of giving us a kind of "second Fenichel". All the same, we must also mention the work's disadvantage: its having been split up between four authors, which, incidentally, is now a trend in scientific literature, in order to ensure maximum objectivity and multi-angular approach; this, however, often gets stuck, through excessive splitting, in a chaotic overlapping of topics, lack of coherence and, notwithstanding the presentation of interesting personal views, lack of a unitary representation. Loch's work, with the comparatively low number of collaborators has successfully managed to avoid this trap through the exemplary self-discipline of the four coauthors: Loch himself, the editor, on metapsychology, H. Roskamp on the psycho-analytical theory of neurosis, P. Kutter on the specific forms of neurotic and psychotic illnesses, and W. Wesiack on psychosomatic medicine. Although, on the whole, a good editorial policy has been followed and the whole book has been well planned, it is nevertheless to be deplored that though there is a short, not entirely adequate, chapter at the end of each section on therapy, the book lacks an essential section on the psycho-analytic method itself and on psycho-analytical technique as such, although this is an integral part of the theory and, conversely, the theory of psycho-analysis is intrinsically linked with the method of its application.

Self-Realization and Social Definition Two Aspects of Identity Formation11

Norman Tabachnick

Norman Tabachnick

Norman Tabachnick
LOS ANGELES
435 North Roxbury Drive
Beverly Hills, California.

Norman Tabachnick  

SUMMARY

Human identity is postulated as resulting from the integration of two frames of reference called self-realization and social definition. These forces are examined in terms of their early developmental precursors, and some comments of other writers referring to them are discussed. The hypothesis is presented that identity problems result from a disequilibrium between the two frames of reference.

responseSet.title: Normality and Pathology in Childhood: Assessments of Development
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Normality and Pathology in Childhood: Assessments of Development

Margaret Mead

Margaret Mead

Margaret Mead  

By Anna Freud (New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1965; London: Hogarth 1966. Pp. 272+xii. $5.00. 35s.)

The Family and Individual Development. By D. W. Winnicott (London: Tavistock; New York: Basic Books, 1965. Pp. 181+viii. $5.00. 30s.)

A specialist in another field approaches such works as these with a tremendous sense of humility, a humility which hopefully will match the diffidence with which these two masters of the study of children assess their own contributions to the facilitation of normal development and provision for the results of pathology, both in the individual child and in the family within which the pathology develops.

Miss Freud's formulations have been such an integral part of my own thinking for so many years that I find in this book a vivid illustration of her theoretical path since she presented a "picture of childhood" as seen by a psycho-analyst in the first Handbook of Child Psychology,1 through The Ego and the Mechanisms of Defence2 which reached me in the midst of my field work in Bali and helped to organize my comparative observations on character formation in different cultures, to the inclusive sweep of the present work. I am of course only competent to deal with these subject matters within this general cross-cultural context, seeing them both as a general frame-work for thinking about human development in all cultures, and as the outgrowth of the special discipline of psycho-analysis within our own historical tradition.

These two books, each written out of serenity and many years of experience with the problems of infancy and childhood within psycho-analytic theory, complement each other in many ways. Dr Winnicott begins where Miss Freud ends, in that he takes the whole framework which she presents in such clear outline for granted, and devotes himself to the dimensions of maturation, the child's urge to grow, and the maternal environment within which that growth normally takes place, or the therapeutic environment, supportive or directly analytic, within which the therapist repairs the damage that has been done by the absence of an "ordinary good mother". Much of the material in Miss Freud's book has of course been presented in greater detail in the articles and lectures which have intervened since the publication of The Ego and the Mechanisms of Defence almost thirty years ago. Without a familiarity with this material it would be impossible to follow Winnicott's argument, although the general outlines of his humane reliance on the nurturing drives in female members of his own culture—he deals with no other—would of course shine through. Miss Freud's emphasis on the over-riding importance of the impulse towards growth and Winnicott's emphasis on the child's "innate tendency towards development" mirror each other although the specific content of the two books is very different. Both books are also concerned with the role of the analyst, and especially of the child analyst, Miss Freud more with specific questions of technique, Winnicott more with the resonance of analytic therapy in the practice of what he calls "social work", supportive therapy of an exceedingly delicate sort. With Winnicott the more optimistic and the more prepared to deal with the whole of the outside world, right up to the problems of democratic government, and Miss Freud preserving her scrupulous sense of areas where analysis may and had best not venture, the two authors provide an ethical as well as a theoretical platform for the care of children: both children who are developing in a healthy fashion and those whose development is so pathological that they themselves, their parents, and other children may have to be heavily protected by protracted therapy or institutional care.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Relationships of Infantile Self-Boundary Development to Depression11

Irene Fast

Irene Fast

Irene Fast
Department of Psychology, University of Michigan
Ann Arbor, Michigan

Irene Fast  

SUMMARY

Some implications of the onset of ambivalence in the second six months of life have been related to characteristics of depression in adulthood. A salient development at the onset of ambivalence is seen as the radical restructuring of the self-boundary. Initially all benign experience is within the self-boundary, and all negative experience outside it and ego-alien. At the point of successful resolution both positive and negative self representations must be within the self-boundary but that boundary now excludes both positive and negative representations of other persons and of the non-human environment. Depressive symptoms are related to the inappropriate continuation of the primitive good-bad boundary; inadequate establishment

265

of self-other boundaries; faulty development of the sense of self, of the ego-alien and of separate-but-related; unstable internal structuring of self and of others and the world; and an experienced helpless dependence on another for one's functioning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Family and Individual Development

By D. W. Winnicott. (London: Tavistock; New York: Basic Books, 1965. Pp. 181+viii. 30s. $5.00.)

Elisabeth Geleerd

Elisabeth Geleerd

Elisabeth Geleerd  

We are accustomed to describing and reading about child development entirely from the child's point of view. Thus we will think of the child's mind from birth on in terms of the psychosexual stages of development, the development of object relationships, development of ego functions, precursors of superego, and the development of the superego proper, etc. One might tend to describe the environment, the reality situation, as a given with which the developing child interacts. In The Family and Individual Development Winnicott deals extensively with this environment. Not only are the members of the immediate family important, but all who will be in contact with the child during his development from infancy through adolescence. In early infancy the environment is the mother. As the child grows older, and his perceptions and interests widen, the family becomes the environment. There are ever-widening circles; school, friends, and finally the community, are the environment.

Even before the birth of her child the mother, who is then a complete unit with her child, has to be seen by the obstetrician, and maybe a nurse or social worker. At the birth, there may be a midwife, then a paediatrician, the staff of well-baby clinics, teachers, welfare officers, etc., etc., who will be contacted by the mother. Thus she and her child will become part of an enlarged family, and the knowledge and behaviour of any of these people may be crucial, in a positive or a negative way, at a certain time in the life of the child. It is to all of these persons that Winnicott addresses himself.

The book is a compilation of seventeen lectures and papers, each one dealing with an aspect of this interplay between outer and inner forces, but with the emphasis on the influence of the outer forces. The eighteenth, final, chapter deals with some thoughts on the meaning of democracy. Because the author avoids psycho-analytic terminology, using laymen's vocabulary, the content of this book can reach a large group of readers. Not that this is an easy book. It is tightly written and many a single sentence reveals a complete theory.

responseSet.title: Sidney Tarachow—1908–1965
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sidney Tarachow—1908–196511

John Frosch

John Frosch

John Frosch  

After lingering for one month between life and death following a heart attack, Sidney Tarachow died on 6th December 1965 at the age of 57. Those who were close to him experienced each day the fluctuations of mood which accompanied every sign of improvement or worsening, until quite suddenly and quietly, just as the Fall meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association were drawing to a close, he breathed his last.

It is difficult for one who was close to Sidney Tarachow to appraise objectively his position in the field of psycho-analysis apart from him as a human being. His versatility, variety of interests, and indefatigable curiosity led him into activities of such dimensions that one cannot hope to do justice in so brief a statement to the many-sidedness of his personality.

Born in Russia, and brought to America as a child where he spent his developmental years in Milwaukee, he received first his medical degree from Marquette University, and subsequently a Doctorate of Medical Science from the College of Physicians and Surgeons of Columbia University. He trained in Neurology, and then worked in his earlier years with problems in delinquency. While associated in a visiting capacity at the Mount Sinai and Beth Israel Hospitals, he undertook his psycho-analytic training at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

This was a period of Sturm und Drang in psycho-analytic circles, which finally exploded into the formation of dissenting groups, which broke away from the New York Psychoanalytic Institute. Sidney, with many of his friends, joined one of these splinter groups. However, he slowly came to realize that for him this had been an error. It is certainly a measure of his stature as a man that he had no hesitation in returning to the New York Psychoanalytic Institute as a candidate, despite the fact that he had already reached faculty status at another institution. When he returned we had many discussions about his search to find himself analytically. It was then that I came to appreciate the basic integrity, honesty, and fearlessness with which he could examine himself and candidly acknowledge errors in judgment. These qualities, together with his delightful sense of humour, wit and endless store of jokes helped to build between us a bond which wore well over the years.

47 Frognal, London, N.W.3.

D. Meltzer  

Your interest and cooperation are invited in an experiment in scientific communication among those psycho-analysts who have a strong interest in the developments that have sprung from the work of Melanie Klein. While the official journals and publishers offer ample scope for contributions to the literature of psycho-analysis, international communication about problems of current interest in regard to the clinical work of psycho-analysis is virtually nonexistent. The tendency for isolated regional development is therefore checked only by the occasional visiting lecturer and returning student. Personal relations on an international level are restricted to senior people who are thrown together mainly by political and organization problems. As a result, little opportunity exists for young analysts to present their work, and they are thus deprived of one of the major vehicles of technical development.

With these problems in mind, and with the added intention of providing a means whereby clinical information may accumulate for future use, the C.D.S. is put forward for consideration. It is a service not a journal, and is offered as follows:

i. It is distributed to its clients as received, without selection or editing, in numbers of fifty pages each.

ii. It contains the following types of contribution:

a. clinical data notes, not longer than 4, 000 words, in English;

b. statements of clinical problems with requests for information from other analysts, not longer than 2, 000 words;

c. therapeutic results—clients are supplied with a diagnostic data sheet, to be drawn up by the directors, by means of which they are invited to report privately their clinical results. This aspect of the service is optional.

iii. This service is operated by its directors as a cooperative. An annual statement of accounts will be produced and the deficit will be equally divided among its clients.

The service commenced in September, at present serving a clientele of eighty analysts from twelve countries, who have been privately circularized because of their known interest in the work of Melanie Klein.

Object Relations in the Psychiatric Hospital11

Michael Woodbury

Michael Woodbury

Michael A. Woodbury
Soisy, France
Hôpital Psychiatrique
L'Eau Vive
91-Soisy-sur-Seine.

Michael Woodbury  

I have described elsewhere (1964) the seven-year study of a psychiatric ward conducted at Chestnut Lodge. In summary, a total of fifty psychotic female patients, mostly schizophrenic with similar types and degree of illness, were treated and studied in great detail over a seven-year period during which all of them were in individual psycho-analytically oriented psychotherapy and under my administration. The observations on the interactions between all the members of this community (staff, patients and their families) were then analysed retrospectively in order to detect any repetitive patterns with more generalized theoretical implications.

The history of this ward clearly divided itself into three eras, each with its own distinctive philosophy of treatment on the part of the staff, and different prevalent symptoms on the part of the patients:

We found a correlation between a milieu emphasizing limit-setting through the threat of exclusion or abandonment and the preponderance of regressed symptoms during Era I. During Era II, self-destructiveness increased notably in an atmosphere of permissiveness in which the patient had no clear limits set and was threatened with boundarylessness. The emphasis on shifting self-responsibility upon the patients during Era III correlated with an increased amount of acting out such as unauthorized absences as well as more numerous discharges to the community (Woodbury, 1964).

In this paper I intend to examine the pattern of object relations between the psychotic patients, their families, and the staff from the point of view of the Ward Administrator who has decision-making but no individual psychotherapeutic responsibilities on the patients living on his unit. Although this study was done in a Clinic with an experience of more than thirty years in the psycho-analytic treatment of psychotics, similar findings have been obtained in markedly different settings: Saint Elizabeth's Hospital, an eight-thousand-bed Federal Psychiatric Hospital in Washington D.C., and Les Rives de Prangins, a private Clinic in Switzerland.

Sense, Affect, and Image in Development of the Symbolic Process11

Itamar Yahalom

Itamar Yahalom

Itamar Yahalom
Reiss-Davis Child Study Center
9760 West Pico Boulevard
Los Angeles, Calif 90035

Itamar Yahalom  

Nature speaks to us, first of all, through our senses; the forms and qualities we distinguish, remember, imagine, or recognize are symbols of entities which exceed and outlive our momentary experience.
—Susanne K. Langer:
Philosophy in a New Key

Every therapist knows that his patients teach him more than he teaches them. He also knows, though he sometimes forgets, that theory must continually be retempered and reshaped in the crucible of the therapeutic relationship. One deeply disturbed six-year-old forced her therapist and his supervisor to explore in new depth the nature of stereotypic repetitive behaviour and the roles of monsters and other psychotic defences in severe disturbances.

Ruth D forced these explorations by insisting on play-acting endlessly with the characters in the Oz stories and with members of television's macabre Addams Family. She set the stage, assigned the roles, ordering her therapist to assume a succession of monster roles. In so doing he was forced to re-explore his own therapeutic attitude. He came to see and respect the fact that Ruth (and other patients) wanted the therapist to become a willing servant, one who serves faithfully and with wholehearted devotion. Ruth spelled out the servant's duties in astounding detail.

CASE MATERIAL

As Ruth Entered Therapy

Of course Ruth wove into her play-acting emotionally charged material from her own life. Her parents, especially her mother, continually pressurized the child to be "normal", to go to a "normal school", to be a social asset rather than a liability. Recordings of her therapy starkly revealed external and internal tensions. They showed the stages through which her therapy went and her expectations concerning her therapist. Whether she play-acted with witches, Oz characters, or Addams Family characters, Ruth kept repeating one theme: "Help me. Without your help I have no way of coping with pressures which I feel to be over-whelming. Help me survive these pressures."

When Ruth first came to the Treatment Center she displayed her intense motor and verbal activity. She tore her clothes, threw paint around, and leapt over objects. She grimaced and twiddled with her fingers, tapping parts of her body and the furniture in a stereotyped, repetitive pattern. She made noises and used some words. She could sometimes be controlled by the therapist verbally or physically and she soon learned to call the Director for help. His appearance would bring an abrupt cessation of what she called her "wild" behaviour.

Thought Disorder11

J. Greene

J. Greene

J. C. Greene
26 Fitzjohn's Avenue
London, N.W.3

J. Greene  

… what was required was discipline. An officer who aspires to be the psychiatrist in charge of a rehabilitation wing … will realize that it is his task to produce self-respecting men socially adjusted to the community and, therefore, willing to accept its responsibilities whether in peace or war.

—Bion (1961)

This paper is an attempt towards a critique of Bion's unusually suggestive work, focussing in particular on his concept of thought disorder and on his effort to think about psycho-analytic thinking. I investigate the suggestiveness to decide whether it is as fecund as it seems exciting; but I intend to leave aside any discussion of the concept of personal identity, although the most fundamental examination of Bion's account of thought disorder as a form of dissociation presupposes clarification of what is meant by association as incarnated by a person.

Bion's theory of thought disorder, I argue, is like the "scientific deductive system" he calls Dada (Bion, 1962): i.e. it has become a thing-in-itself, without connexion with the phenomena from which it is supposedly deduced; or, rather, the phenomena are seen in terms of what it is claimed is the scientific deductive system abstracted from them, and this deductive system is grounded on a false premiss. This does not, of course, entail ubiquitous rejection of all his descriptive inferences (such as projective identification, etc.), but it does of his explanation of that part of the patient's behaviour which is responsible for such attributions being, in his view, appropriate. His concept of thought disorder, I believe, transforms the analyst (or psychiatrist) into an appendage to a common fantasy object (in a way analogous to that, for instance, in which the Jews in Germany in the 1930s were constituted as such an object by the anti-semites), whose tromp l'oeil existence is maintained partly by means of the custom of regarding psycho-analytic theory, when convenient, as a branch of biology. I here argue that thought disorder in a psycho-analytic session is either (a) the disordering of the patient's thoughts and perceptions by the analyst, or (b) adequate representation of a patient's catastrophic situation by means of catastrophic language, or (c) linguistic innovation by the patient (free-er free associations than most patients are capable of);2 and I refer to empirical evidence, primarily in the form of the tape-recordings made by Laing and Esterson (1964), to support the first two of these contentions as well as a fourth, (d), that overlaps with them: namely, that thought disorder is a strategy employed in an impossible situation. But whether it is (a) or (b) or (c) or (d) or any combination of these, it cannot (logically) be explained biologically by reference to a putative death instinct, because it is not the symptom of a disease (when disease = biologically dysfunctional).

[[RunningHead]]

Sigmund Freud and the Jewish Mystical Tradition

By David Bakan. (London: Bailey Bros. & Swinfen; New York: Schocken Books. 1965. Pp. 326. xxi 17s.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

This is the first Schocken paperback edition of a book published by the Van Nostrand Company in 1958.

The author is dissastisfied with the explanations so far offered of the factors which enabled Freud to create psycho-analysis. For him the major question is: Against what backdrop of the history of ideas shall we place these momentous contributions of Freud?. This book provides Bakan's evidence that the answer is to be found in the tradition of Jewish mysticism i.e. "that Freud's repeated affirmation of his Jewish identity had greater significance for the development of psycho-analysis than is usually recognized." As he says "In the course of this essay, we have attempted in various ways to show the possibility of an essentially unrevealed and perhaps unconscious Jewish base to the developments in Freud's thought." In the transition from neurology to psychology "Freud, so to speak, made contact with the Jewish mystical tradition as it was part and parcel of his personality and culture."

It seems odd at first to associate Freud and mysticism but it has to be remembered that his birth-place was a centre of Chassidism (the then current form of Jewish mysticism) and that he read the Bible with his father, as is shown by the letter accompanying the father's gift of this particular Bible to Freud on his 35th birthday. The religious atmosphere of the home (Catholic nurse notwithstanding) must have had considerable effect and, so far as intellectual development is concerned, the ideas expressed in the home circle must have provided Freud with his first food for thought.

The "various ways" used by the author cannot be detailed in a short notice. They could be employed only by someone well-versed in Jewish mysticism. Fortunately for the probably ignorant non-Jewish reader, a brief but informative historical sketch is provided. Even Ernest Jones must be regarded as among the uninstructed in this field, since he wrote in the Biography "When Freud spoke of his having been greatly influenced by his early reading of the Bible he can only have meant in an ethical sense, in addition to his historical interest.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Revolution. Sigmund Freud's Life and Achievement

By Marthe Robert. Translated by Kenneth Morgan. (London: Allen & Unwin, 1967. Pp. 396. 50s.)

Katherine Jones

Katherine Jones

Katherine Jones  

This is an attractive, interesting and well-written book. In it Mme Robert traces Freud's development, his childhood, youth, and old age in a fair and even admiring manner. We are left in no doubt that here we have the history of a great man whose achievements, as is always the case with great achievements, were not easily obtained. His struggles, both with poverty and later on with opposition from enemies and even friends are outlined with sympathy and lucidity.

This is not the same as saying that Mme Robert's book gives us much that is new. She is greatly indebted to Ernest Jones and acknowledges this debt by forty-eight references to his name. She also quotes long passages from his book, and a number of letters which can be found in the same Biography. However, it is ten years since the last of the three volumes of the official Biography was finished, and so there is perhaps a case for a new book for a different class of readers. That this need was widely felt is borne out by the appearance in 1961 of the abridged edition of Jones's Biography and by the spate of books on Sigmund Freud for all conditions of people.

I was reminded of Goethe's aphorism that when everything has been thought of, the thing is to think it over again. This Mme Robert certainly does. Her recital of Freud's life is as interesting as it should be for all who admire the great man. She stops short of giving a characterization of his friends and followers, but might say that this is not within the scope of her book. She gives much intelligent thought to Freud's works and has done a good amount of rethinking in this respect. She also gives us excerpts and quotations from some of the letters that appeared after the official Biography was written, which are very welcome. There are quite a number of misspellings and other errors which I find irritating in the book, and it is to be hoped that these will be remedied for another edition.

For a public that does not venture into reading the three-volume, or even the abridged edition, of Jones's biography, or for her compatriots, Mme Robert's book must be very welcome. It is naturally of less importance for trained and practising analysts in this country; though nevertheless a very creditable achievement.

[[RunningHead]]

The Separation of Management from the Therapeutic Setting in a Paranoid Patient11

Alfred Flarsheim

Alfred Flarsheim

Alfred Flarsheim
25 East Washington Street.
Chicago, Illinois. 60602

Alfred Flarsheim  

I will describe certain technical procedures used in the treatment of a paranoid adolescent. This was an attempt to construct an environment within which potentially disruptive forces, stimulated by the patient's relationship with his mother, could be turned to therapeutic use.

The patient is a severely disturbed young man, and from the standpoint of life adjustment, the treatment was not a "success story", but it offers us an opportunity to discuss very important questions.

First I will delineate the therapeutic setting, and then the way in which this paranoid patient made use of it. The following phases could be separated:

i. Localization and control of his paranoid expectations as a result of the treatment alliance.

ii. Active staging of an assault by his mother on his therapy—the kind of assault which he had previously had to undergo alone.

iii. Regression to an organized dependent state, after his therapy survived. During the regression, he (a) developed painful somatic symptoms which led to our discovery that (b) he assumed that I was providing magical protection and assistance, and (c) he accepted and utilized physical care in a nurturing, sustaining relationship with his mother which was a new achievement for them both.

iv. Emergence from the regression, with the retention of some of the vividness of perception and feeling that he had first experienced in the painful somatic symptoms.

ESTABLISHMENT OF THERAPEUTIC SETTING

First Contact with the Case

My first contact with the case was a telephone call from the patient's mother. She requested that I see her 17-year-old son who had been diagnosed as a schizophrenic while currently hospitalized. I offered to see him in my office, although she had expected that I would see him in a hospital.

She described her son as very disturbed and at the same time as conforming and capable. As so often in the late adolescent, both were true, i.e. that he could at times adjust to the outer world despite severe inner disturbance. She stressed that she wanted her son "to get a good job", and therefore wanted the "best treatment" for him. Then she asked: "What if he won't come in to see you?

[[RunningHead]]

The Pathology and Treatment of Sexual Deviation

Edited by Ismond Rosen (London: Oxford Univ. Press, 1964. Pp. 510. 50 s.)

V. Smirnoff

V. Smirnoff

V. Smirnoff  

This collection of essays from a wide range of disciplines had its origins in a Congress on the subject of sexual deviation held under the auspices of the Portman Clinic in London.

The first essay is one by Professor Tinbergen, in which he demonstrates his method of observing animal behaviour, taking the black-headed gull species as his example. The method is determined by the nature of the observables: movements of animals, circumstances by which they are elicited, their function or survival value. Tinbergen states that he deliberately refrains from any attempt at making guesses about the nature of the possible subjective phenomena which may be experienced by his subjects, and from "making any but the most tentative comparison with Man." In man, both the selective responsiveness to outside stimuli and the motor patterns by which a given type of motivation expresses itself can undergo great changes during a lifetime; aggression for instance can be expressed in various ways—by "a seemingly friendly letter, doodling during a committee meeting, or lighting a cigarette". No such variety exists with the black-headed gull; aggression, i.e. "a behaviour involving approach or pursuit and attempts at administering stimuli to another animal which make it withdraw or flee", is almost the same all through all members of the species, with little individual difference. The same can be said about "fear" or "sexual behaviour".

Psycho-analysis has reached a point where it can use new vistas; and there is no doubt that the ethological notions of displacement activities, of motivational conflict, or selective responsiveness, which Tinbergen presents, shed a new light on our thinking in terms of behaviour. It also raises the important question at the very start of whether analysts are concerned with behaviour or with signalling systems, as they evolve from conflictual situations, and as such may be conceived of as one episode in the process of symbolic elaboration.

From the biological scene comes a well-documented paper by Richard P. Michael on the "Biological Factors in the Organization and Expression of Sexual Behaviour". The paper reflects the fantastic amount of ingenious experimental work that has been done lately in this field, but this seems to have yielded few factual conclusions; the strong desire to build on "solid ground", i.e. to find some organic impairment, some endocrine disorder, some constitutional factor to account for variations in sexual behaviour, has not been fulfilled.

The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud

Translated from the German under the general editorship of James Strachey, in collaboration with Anna Freud, assisted by Alix Strachey and Alan Tyson. Editorial assistant: Angela Richards. Vol. I (1886–1899) Pre-Psycho-Analytic Publications and Unpublished Drafts. (London: Hogarth Press and Institute of Psycho-Analysis. Vol. I, 1966, pp. xxvi + 430. £50 the set of 24 vols; sold only in sets.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

This volume includes the earlier writings of Freud that have any relation to his development of psycho-analysis, including drafts of papers published during the years 1866–1899. The papers actually published during this period are given in Vols. II and III of this edition. Since the final volume of the series, still to come, will contain only a full bibliography and indexes, the publication of this volume has rightly been the occasion for widespread congratulation of James Strachey and his co-workers on the completion of their onerous but invaluable task.

The volume opens with a General Preface. The editor describes the scope, plan, and sources of the Standard Edition, framed with the "serious student" in mind, for whose use the different types of commentary and notes are intended. A section on translation discusses the problem of rendering "Freud, the whole of Freud, and nothing but Freud" not only accurately (this involved many sub-problems such as untranslatable terms) but in a style of English that did justice to Freud's own fine writing. Despite the almost over-modest dedication that calls the edition a "blurred reflection", readers will surely agree that truly phenomenal and exemplary success has been achieved in both these aims. The "Acknowledgments" recount the difficulties that beset the launching of the enterprise and of the crucial help given by the United States, e.g. by Dr John Murray and others, both American and English, including Anna Freud. There is a special tribute to Alix Strachey. A list of technical terms calling for comment is appended.

The text itself begins, logically enough, with Freud's "Report on my studies in Paris and Berlin", since the visit to Paris was the turning-point in his career. He himself rated his contact with Charcot as the most important part of his experiences there and it was this contact that first stimulated his interest in psychopathology, as distinct from neurology. He left Paris as "Charcot's unqualified admirer". Though he came to differ from him in later years, he never lost his good opinion of the man himself or of his work. This is evident from his Preface to his translation of Charcot's book on diseases of the nervous system (this volume, p. 21) and in the later (1893) Obituary (Vol. III, p. 9). In the paper on a male hysteric that follows the Preface, the case is presented on purely Charcotian lines. By 1892–94 the footnotes to his preface to Charcot's Tuesday lectures (Vol. I, p. 131) show that he has become much more critical. The association with Breuer had intervened and Freud had already concluded that hysterics suffered from reminiscences and could be cured by catharsis.
