responseSet.title: The Reality of the Psycho-Analytic Relationship and Situation in the Handling of Transference-Resistance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Reality of the Psycho-Analytic Relationship and Situation in the Handling of Transference-Resistance11

Alan Roland

Alan Roland

Alan Roland
241 West 12th Street
New York, N.Y. 10014

Alan Roland  

The kind of cases I would like to discuss are, in the main, patients with severe neurotic character disturbances. The subject is that delicate relationship between the real relationship between patient and analyst, and the intense transference relationship which usually develops in psycho-analytic therapy. My thesis, which I would like to illustrate with clinical material and discuss theoretically, is that a real, reparative relationship must be developed and cultivated in psycho-analysis with these patients, so that character transference resistances may be handled in the earlier stages of therapy, and later regressive transferences, or the transference neurosis, may be fostered and eventually resolved before becoming an unworkable transference-resistance. Further, there may also be reality aspects of the normal, expectant psycho-analytic relationship which may prove a hindrance to the development of a reparative, real relationship; which must be developed in order to have a working (Greenson, 1965) or therapeutic alliance (Zetzel, 1956) to handle earlier and later transference resistances. A further corollary is that unless sufficient attention is paid to both the real and transference aspects of the paradoxical psycho-analytic relationship with severe neurotic patients, grave difficulties arise. I would like to add parenthetically that much that I am outlining already occurs in what I believe to be good analytic work, but that I have rarely seen it discussed well conceptually in the psycho-analytic literature.

My main case illustration is that of Martha who came to me in her early 20s from a socially prominent Mid-Western family. Early familial relationships consisted of a social-form driven mother, unresponsive and un-empathetic to her daughter and sons, her character consisting much more of ground than figure, similar to Vieulliard's painting of the artist's mother fading into the wallpaper at the Modern Museum in New York City. This relationship was somewhat mitigated by the presence of a robust maid from the labouring class, and a father devoted to his children, but who added further problems because of an authoritarian rigidity of opinions, and an unbending will. The girl bounded from her family to New York City, but except for an intense though by no means satisfactory affair, she led an uninvolved, unpeopled life, often giving herself over to days of inactivity and depression. She came to me after a year's hiatus from another therapist, an apparently unsatisfactory relationship shipwrecked on the shoals of her uninvolvement.

Dream Form and Strength of Impulse in Dreams of Falling and Other Dreams of Descent11

Leon Saul and George Curtis

George Curtis

Leon J. Saul
George C. Curtis
275 Highland Avenue
Media, Pa., 19063.

George Curtis  

SUMMARY

Of the groups studied statistically by various authors, dreams of falling occurred in 52.3 to 88 per cent of the members at some time in their lives.

A variety of meanings for these dreams have been suggested in the literature, but only rarely on the basis of psycho-analytic analysis of specific dreams with adequate associations.

In our analytic experience dreams of falling are not an isolated type but share one of their frequent latent meanings with other dreams of descent.

This meaning, based on study of life situations and associations to dream elements is the impulse to give up effort or to abandon, flee from, or fall away from responsibility, combined with spite and hostility directed more or less against the self. When a housewife's frustrations mounted until she had rages at her husband, drank excessively, wanted to give up completely and had strong suicidal urges, then she had almost nightly dreams of falling from which she awoke in panic. When a man got into rages which made him feel powerful urges to leave his wife, children, and job, he had anxiety dreams of falling. But patients who were merely temporarily overworked and were not threatened with serious regression or excessive rage, dreamed of small falls or gentle descents.

Thus, dreams of falling are seen as only one end of a series or spectrum of dreams of descent.

The qualitative form of the manifest dream varies with the quantitative balance between id and ego and superego forces.

Height may be symbolic of status, success, accomplishment and the like, because these qualities are associated with the height of the parent, in contrast to the lowness of the child.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent Depression11

Sandor Lorand

Sandor Lorand

Sandor Lorand
40 West 59th Street
New York 19, N.Y.

Sandor Lorand  

Introduction

Sixty years ago, Freud (1905) described detachment from parental authority as the most significant—and the most painful—psychic achievement of adolescence. This detachment involves a most difficult struggle; it is indeed of crucial significance in determining whether he will fall within the range of what we consider normal or whether he will be maladjusted. In this essay I shall be focusing on the dynamics of this struggle which appears with dramatic clarity in cases of depressive illness.

The depressed adolescent repeatedly complains of a confused sense of identity; he is apathetic at certain times; over-active at others; at all times, however, he fears criticism. He fears a further loss of his already highly diminished self-esteem and is convinced that he is an unloved and unlovable "nobody". The sense of isolation produced by these feelings becomes increasingly more tormenting; the patient is unable to relinquish his dependent relationship with his parents; he is incapable of relinquishing the past; he is unable to go forward into a "future". He is unable to form new object relationships. The many problems centering on ego identity interfere with further development of the ego towards maturity.

His troubled ego attempts to control the strong instinctual forces and channelize them into a more mature direction, but fails; his inability to function serves as a "realistic" if not desirable means of maintaining his ties to the family.

He cannot adjust to new reality demands; he cannot comply with the superego demands. His superego elements show inconsistent strivings, which are also responsible for his faulty identification, his distorted and confused self-image. His ego and superego are in continual conflict, resulting in feelings of guilt.

Clinical investigations focus attention on the functions of superego manifestations concerning conscience, ethical and moral standards, and problems concerning ego-ideal defects originating in infantile pathological problems of identification. The need for compliance to the demands of the superego and to reality, on the one hand, and the rebellion of the ego against these pressures and demands, on the other hand, produces a steady conflict situation with feelings of guilt which sustains the depression.

[[RunningHead]]

Silence as a Technical Parameter in Psycho-Analysis

Roy Calogeras

Roy Calogeras

Roy C. Calogeras
155 East 73rd Street
New York, N.Y. 10021
U.S.A.

Roy Calogeras  

SUMMARY

The primary purpose of this paper was to elucidate the meaning of silence when used as a technical parameter in analysis. A brief overview, in terms of the different periods of psycho-analytic theory and technical development of the relevant research and the emergence of new conceptions of silence was made. This was followed by the presentation of case material highlighting such aspects as basic role and precipitating conditions, criteria for introduction, vicissitudes encountered and the ultimate resolution, when silence as a fundamental modification (parameter) in treatment was introduced. Implications stemming from the findings suggested that "parameterized silence" was a valid procedure to be used under certain carefully specified conditions as a kind of "lend-lease" arrangement with the patient—thereby allowing a temporary by-passing of seemingly insurmountable resistance—and thus enabling her to recoup and integrate her psychic forces for the main efforts later on in the analysis. It was concluded, among other things, that analytic silence when used in this fashion may greatly assist the analyst in deepening his understanding, both technically and theoretically, of the nature and intricacies of the basic psycho-analytic model; that its proper handling could well be a crucial nexus in enhancing the therapeutic alliance; and that certain tentative criteria for introducing silence as a parameter could be stated.

responseSet.title: Distinctions Between Fantasy and Reality Elements in Memory and Reconstruction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Distinctions Between Fantasy and Reality Elements in Memory and Reconstruction

Oscar Sachs

Oscar Sachs

Oscar Sachs
1148 Fifth Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028.

Oscar Sachs  

The difficulties we frequently encounter in distinguishing between fantasy and reality elements in the "memories" of traumatic events brought to us by our patients, often seem insurmountable. The problem revolves in part about our own credulity, and as such, mirrors the discovery which played such an important role in the development of psycho-analysis. Freud's realization that many of the childhood seduction "memories" which he had originally believed, were in fact fantasies growing out of developmental conflicts and desires, led him to draw the conclusion that "as far as neurosis was concerned, psychical reality was of more importance than material reality" (Freud, 1925). With the expanded knowledge and understanding of the ubiquitous fantasies associated with infantile stages of development, our interest and accent has been placed more and more on fantasy, perhaps at times to the neglect of the accompanying events. Pragmatically almost, there seems to have developed an attitude that it made little difference whether a remembered traumatic event occurred or was fantasied; the latter, subjective, drive-dependent, experience came to be accorded the primary aetiological significance.

Although many of the interpretations and reconstructions we use during any analysis are often at best but approximations, (Reider, 1953) yet they exert dynamic and lasting effects upon the patient. We deal for the most part with "complex patterns" since, as Kris (1956) wrote,

the material of actual occurrences of things as they happen is constantly subjected to the selective scrutiny of memory under the guide of the inner constellation. What we here call selection is itself a complex process. Not only were the events loaded with meaning when they occurred; each later stage of the conflict pattern may endow part of the events, or of their elaboration with added meaning. But these processes are repeated throughout many years of childhood and adolescence and finally integrated into the structure of the personality. They are moulded as it were into patterns, and it is with these patterns rather than with the events, that the analyst deals.

[[RunningHead]]

Thomas Woodrow Wilson: A Psychological Study

By Sigmund Freud and William C. Bullitt. (London: Weidenfeld & Nicolson. Pp. 265, 36s.)1

Geoffrey Gorer

Geoffrey Gorer

Geoffrey Gorer  

The motive inspiring this distasteful essay in posthumous denigration can probably be found in the public letter Bullitt wrote to President Wilson in May 1919, when he resigned from the American delegation to the Paris peace conference. Part of it says:

I was one of the millions who trusted confidently and implicitly in your leadership and believed that you would take nothing less than a "permanent peace" based upon "unselfish and unbiased justice" … I am sorry that you did not fight our fight to the finish and that you had so little faith in the millions of men, like myself, in every nation who had faith in you.

Bullitt clearly thought he had been personally betrayed; in the long interruption of his professional career entailed by his resignation he must have been simmering with indignation and probably found comfort in the idea of a book which would show the world that his former idol had feet of clay—up to the eyebrows.

This work would seem to be almost completely worthless as history. It is totally undocumented and records only those facts which show Wilson in an unfavourable light. On the basis of this book it is impossible to understand how the governors of Princeton or the electors of New Jersey and of the United States could have chosen and re-chosen so inadequate a man for offices of the highest responsibility. Thoughts and feelings are imputed to Wilson in the fashion of mediocre historical novelists.
